meta.title: Scind-ahead: capabilities specified in Scind, unexercised in Xcind
meta.method: Five parallel subagents by Scind capability cluster (state/generation, networking/gateway, shell-integration, targeting/CLI-semantics, roadmap-futures), each quoting Scind canon and searching Xcind bin/ + lib/xcind/ + test/ + behaviors. Overlapping findings deduplicated (manifest, flavors, SNI/protocol, port-scan).

labels.NOT-IMPLEMENTED: Xcind could/should build it; simply hasn't.
labels.IMPLEMENTED-UNTESTED: Code exists but no behavior/test/spec exercises it — correctness unproven (latent-bug risk).
labels.DELIBERATELY-DEFERRED: Xcind consciously scoped it out (for now or forever) → P7 divergence registry.
labels.CANON-OVERREACH: Building Xcind suggests the Scind spec is wrong/gold-plated/unbuildable → change Scind (P6).
labels.ESCALATE: Open question a human must resolve; not yet classifiable (added per human product-call).

meta.human_product_calls_applied: 2026-07-15 — Human product-calls received after the initial pass OVERRIDE the coordinator's independent labels where they conflict (rows tagged "source": "human product-call"): (1) host-gateway container-injection is DELIBERATELY-DEFERRED, NOT canon-overreach — Scind keeps mandating it; (2) Scind reference appendices stay as-is for Scind, a known divergence, not a gap Xcind must close; (3) generated-manifest / shell-integration / stateless model are architectural DELIBERATELY-DEFERRED; (4) the scind-compose shell script is an open question → ESCALATE (SA-0026); (5) verified Xcind .feature files are living-documentation only (NOT executed by make test), and no IMPLEMENTED-UNTESTED / tested-not-a-gap call relied on them — all rest on test/*.sh.
meta.behaviors_feature_files_note: Xcind's engineering/behaviors/*.feature files are NOT executed: Makefile `test` runs only test/test-xcind.sh, test/test-xcind-proxy.sh, test/test-xcind-prompt.sh; no cucumber/bats/.feature runner exists. They are living documentation. All 'exercised'/'tested' determinations in this artifact rest on the test/*.sh shell suites, not on .feature files.

gaps.capability: Options-based targeting by name (--workspace/-w, --app/-a) runnable from anywhere
gaps.scind_promise: Use `--workspace` and `--app` options (not positional arguments) with automatic context detection from current directory. All targeting uses options rather than positional arguments: `scind app status --workspace=dev --app=frontend`.
gaps.evidence: No --workspace/--app/--target flag exists in any bin/xcind-* parser. Xcind targets via (a) upward $PWD walk to nearest .xcind.sh (xcind-compose/xcind-config/xcind-prompt; engineering/specs/context-detection.md:1-32), (b) optional positional [DIR] defaulting to '.' (bin/xcind-application:585-590, xcind-workspace), or (c) XCIND_APP_ROOT override. The mechanism is documented, but there is NO name resolver — you cannot target a workspace/app by name from an arbitrary cwd, and no Xcind ADR records the deviation from Scind ADR-0011.
gaps.recommendation: Xcind's cwd-walk + positional DIR + XCIND_APP_ROOT is an intentional, documented alternate mechanism → P7 divergence. BUT: (1) file an Xcind ADR that records the decision to diverge from ADR-0011 (currently implicit); (2) note the genuine sub-gap 'no target-by-name-from-anywhere' as a possible backlog item if cross-directory targeting is ever wanted.

gaps.capability: Expose resolved host-gateway value as a named *_HOST_GATEWAY environment variable inside containers
gaps.scind_promise: The resolved workstation host value SHOULD also be exposed as an environment variable inside containers: `services.web.environment: - SCIND_HOST_GATEWAY=<resolved_value>`. This enables applications to reference the workstation host programmatically without relying on DNS resolution of host.docker.internal (e.g. Xdebug `client_host=${SCIND_HOST_GATEWAY}`). The variable name is implementation-defined (e.g. XCIND_HOST_GATEWAY for Xcind) but SHOULD follow the SCIND_ prefix convention.
gaps.evidence: CODE DIVE CONFIRMED (xcind-host-gateway-lib.bash:200-224): xcind-host-gateway-hook builds output containing ONLY a `services.<name>.extra_hosts` list with `host.docker.internal:<resolved>` (plus carried-forward existing entries) and writes compose.host-gateway.yaml — there is NO `environment:` block and NO named gateway variable emitted into any container. grep for '_HOST_GATEWAY=' across lib/ and bin/ finds only a SHA cache-input string (xcind-lib.bash:1316), not a container injection. engineering/specs/environment-variables.md never mentions injecting a host-gateway env var. The extra_hosts normalization itself IS built and well-tested (~15 tests, test/test-xcind.sh:3521-3867); only the env-var-exposure half is absent.
gaps.recommendation: PER HUMAN PRODUCT-CALL: Scind KEEPS mandating the *_HOST_GATEWAY env-var exposure in its canon — this is NOT canon-overreach and the Scind spec is NOT to be softened. Xcind knowingly emits extra_hosts only; record as a documented known divergence → P7. Xcind MAY later add `environment: - XCIND_HOST_GATEWAY=<resolved_value>` (value already computed in-hook, small addition unblocking Xdebug/webhook workflows) as an optional backlog item, but its absence is an accepted difference, not a gap Scind must accommodate.

gaps.capability: Shell completion FUNCTION behavior (COMPREPLY generation, docker __complete delegation, directive-bit parsing)
gaps.scind_promise: Completion supports: Command and subcommand names; Flag names and values; Workspace names (from registry); Application names. `scind-compose exec [TAB]` -> php nginx mysql redis (delegated docker compose completion).
gaps.evidence: lib/xcind/xcind-completion-bash.bash:19-99 implements docker `__complete` delegation, directive-bit parsing (error/nospace/nofile via `((directive & N))`, `${lastLine:0:1}` slicing, sed line-delete) and per-command case branches — NONE exercised. test/test-xcind.sh:2936-3002 only asserts the EMITTED script text CONTAINS registration lines (`assert_contains ... 'complete -F ...'`); no test sources the script, sets COMP_WORDS/COMP_CWORD, invokes a completion function, and inspects COMPREPLY. The docker __complete fallback path is the highest latent-bug risk.
gaps.recommendation: LATENT-BUG RISK. Add behavioral tests that source the emitted completion script and drive _xcind_compose_completions / _xcind_config_completions with fabricated COMP_WORDS/COMP_CWORD, asserting COMPREPLY. Xcind promises the behavior via Scind's spec but never verifies it. → Xcind test/behavior backlog.

gaps.capability: Fish shell support (CLI completion + shell-integration setup)
gaps.scind_promise: Fish: `scind completion fish | source` ... | Fish | 3.0+ | ... `scind completion fish` Generate Fish completion script ... `scind init-shell fish >> ~/.config/fish/conf.d/scind.fish`. Scind ships a full fish-setup.fish appendix.
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-config:215-220 accepts only bash|zsh for completion; test/test-xcind.sh:2989-2994 asserts `completion fish` exits non-zero with 'unsupported shell'. No fish file anywhere in lib/ bin/ docs/ (repo-wide `grep -ri fish` empty). docs/guides/tools-ide-integration.md:10-14 documents only bash+zsh. The 'unsupported shell' error is enforcement, not a documented scope decision.
gaps.recommendation: Either add lib/xcind/xcind-completion-fish.fish + accept `completion fish`, OR record a scoping ADR narrowing Xcind to bash/zsh so the omission is an explicit deferral rather than an implicit gap. Currently there is no rationale on record.

gaps.capability: Fail-fast on port-conflict at startup (error when a previously-assigned port became unavailable at `up`)
gaps.scind_promise: If a previously assigned port has become unavailable (e.g. taken by an external process) when `workspace up` runs, Scind fails with a clear error: 'Error: Port conflict detected for frontend ... Port 5432 is assigned to frontend/postgres but is no longer available.'
gaps.evidence: Xcind deliberately does NOT fail on a busy sticky port: lib/xcind/xcind-assigned-lib.bash:789-797 'Sticky hit: trust the TSV. We cannot tell "our own running container" ... source=sticky'. The regression test test/test-xcind-proxy.sh:2753-2772 records the rationale: 'when our own container is running on the sticky port, port-available returns 1 ... The sticky path must trust the TSV and reuse the port — probing and evicting caused ports to flap on every cache miss while the container was up.' A `net.Listen` bind-probe at `up` cannot distinguish the workspace's OWN already-running container (idempotent re-up) from an external process — so Scind's fail-fast would mis-fire on the common re-up case, the exact flapping Xcind hit.
gaps.recommendation: REVERSE LEARNING → change Scind (P6). Scind's startup port-conflict check must exclude ports bound by the workspace's own running containers before declaring a conflict, or idempotent `workspace up` will report false conflicts. Verify Scind's port_inventory + bind-probe actually disambiguates own-container from external-process ownership; if it relies on a bare `net.Listen` it inherits the flapping Xcind proved. Confidence medium: hinges on how Scind's inventory attributes a bound port.

gaps.capability: Explicit primary-export designation (primary: true), implicit-single-primary, and multiple-primaries validation error
gaps.scind_promise: Add a `primary: true` boolean field. Multiple exports, none marked primary: no apex URL generated. Multiple exports, more than one primary: validation error. 'Works correctly with unordered YAML maps (unlike xcind's position-based approach).'
gaps.evidence: lib/xcind/xcind-proxy-lib.bash:806,838,900,1030 — apex anchor is 'the first proxied export' (positional index 0); no `primary` field parsed, no multi-primary validation, no 'no primary -> no apex' path. Xcind's positional rule (ADR-0007, reaffirmed 0018:45-49) is what Scind ADR-0013:9,30,36 explicitly names and rejects.
gaps.recommendation: The designation MECHANISM is deliberately absent (Xcind uses positional first-proxied). CANON-OVERREACH CANDIDATE flagged for P3: Xcind's working positional default suggests Scind's explicit-primary + multi-primary-validation may be gold-plated for the common single/ordered case. The apex DIVERGED conflict adjudication is P3's call (correspondence-map.md:291) — this row only records the missing mechanism and the overreach candidacy; do not resolve here.

gaps.capability: Generated manifest (computed, read-only workspace topology view: hostnames, aliases, env vars)
gaps.scind_promise: Location: {workspace}/.generated/manifest.yaml (gitignored). The manifest is a computed, read-only view of the workspace's current state ... resolved values (hostnames, aliases, environment variables). Purposes: discoverability, tool integration, debugging, and caching (compare manifest against configuration to determine if regeneration is needed).
gaps.evidence: engineering/specs/application-lifecycle.md:12-14 states apps are 'lightweight ... no separate state store, no manifest, and no removal command.' engineering/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md:99-107 lists topology sources (Docker status, .xcind.sh, SHA cache, network inspection) all read 'on demand rather than tracked.' grep for 'manifest' in lib/ and bin/ = zero. The manifest's caching role is served instead by the SHA overlay cache .xcind/generated/{sha}/ (xcind-lib.bash:592-609); its introspection role by `xcind-application status/urls/exports/ports` + `xcind-config --json` (ADR-0015/0017).
gaps.recommendation: Intentional stateless divergence → P7. Xcind derives topology on demand rather than persisting a manifest. No action beyond documenting the divergence.

gaps.capability: Flavors — named variant configs (compose-file sets) + default_flavor + flavor resolution order + `flavor list/show/set` commands + %APPLICATION_FLAVOR% template var
gaps.scind_promise: Flavor: 'A named configuration specifying which compose files to use'. flavors: {lite: {compose_files: [...]}, full: {compose_files: [...]}}; default_flavor. Flavor Resolution Order: 1. CLI flag (--flavor=X) 2. State file (.generated/state.yaml) 3. Application's default_flavor 4. 'default'. `scind flavor list/show/set <flavor>` updates state.yaml and regenerates.
gaps.evidence: No flavor concept in code — 'flavor' hits in bin/lib are only unrelated 'host-flavored' prose (xcind-hostenv-lib.bash:4). No .generated/state.yaml, no default_flavor, no --flavor flag, no `flavor` subcommand. Xcind selects compose files statically via a flat XCIND_COMPOSE_FILES array in .xcind.sh (bin/xcind-application:48,281; xcind-lib.bash:220). Xcind ADR-0005 is derived from Scind's ADR-0005 but DROPPED every flavor row (the 'Active flavor' state row is gone) — a conscious drop, though not stated as an explicit 'we removed flavors' sentence.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Flavors require the persisted state Xcind's stateless model rejects (no state.yaml active-flavor tracking); XCIND_COMPOSE_FILES is the flat equivalent. Soft P6 note: add one explicit sentence to Xcind ADR-0005 recording that flavors were dropped, so the deferral is canonical rather than implicit.

gaps.capability: Port inventory + assigned/unavailable/released status-transition model (first_seen/last_checked, external-process tracking)
gaps.scind_promise: port_inventory: {5432: {status: assigned|unavailable|released, first_seen, last_checked, assignment: {workspace, application, exported_service}}}. Transitions: unavailable->assigned, assigned->released, unavailable->released. Tracks ports taken by external processes, not just Scind's own assignments.
gaps.evidence: Xcind's assigned-ports.tsv schema (xcind-assigned-lib.bash:34) is 'port\tworkspace\tapplication\tservice\tcontainer_port\tapp_path\tassigned_at' — NO status column, NO first_seen/last_checked, NO inventory of non-assigned/external ports. grep 'inventory|unavailable|released|first_seen|last_checked' in lib/ = zero. Xcind tracks ONLY active assignments; nearest analogues are filesystem-based prune (xcind-assigned-lib.bash:579-588, removes rows whose app_path is gone) and manual release (bin/xcind-proxy:455-470).
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Deliberate stateless divergence (ADR-0005). Xcind's path-existence prune + manual release cover the practical GC subset without a persisted port-status inventory.

gaps.capability: Workspace state machine (dormant/starting/running/stopping/stopped/error) + explicit workspace generate/destroy sequences
gaps.scind_promise: Workspaces transition through defined states: dormant, starting, running, stopping, stopped, error. workspace generate produces .generated/*.override.yaml + manifest.yaml. workspace destroy: down --volumes; remove .generated/; prompt before removing app dirs; remove workspace.yaml; release assigned ports; remove from registry.
gaps.source: human product-call (#3: stateless model = architectural divergence)
gaps.evidence: engineering/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md:9 'Xcind workspaces are lightweight ... no separate state management or destruction'; :71 'Removing a Workspace: Simply remove the directory'; :99 'Everything else is runtime state, read from Docker or the filesystem on demand rather than tracked' (running? -> live Docker container status). No standalone generate (implicit via GENERATE hook pipeline, xcind-lib.bash:590-609) and no destroy command. NOTE: Scind's mtime-staleness detection IS functionally matched by Xcind's SHA-cache invalidation (tested, test/test-xcind.sh:1068,1338-1346) — that part is NOT a gap.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Intentional stateless model; Xcind infers status from Docker on demand. See SA-0023 (generate) and SA-0024 (destroy) for the CLI-command facets.

gaps.capability: Port CLI: `port scan` (refresh bind-status inventory) and `port gc` (reclaim assigned-but-unbound ports)
gaps.scind_promise: `scind port scan`: Checks all tracked ports for current bind status; updates port_inventory in global state. `scind port gc`: Scans port inventory for ports marked assigned but not actually bound; releases ports whose workspaces/apps no longer exist; reports actions.
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-proxy subcommands are init/up/down/status/logs/release/prune. `xcind-proxy prune` (__xcind-assigned-prune) removes entries whose app_path no longer exists — the dead-app subset of gc — and runs automatically before proxy up/status/down (bin/xcind-proxy:184,219,287). The 'assigned but not actually bound' reclamation and a persisted bind-status inventory to `scan` do not exist (Xcind keeps no port_inventory; bind status is checked live in `xcind-proxy status`).
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Xcind's auto-running `prune` is the partial gc equivalent; the unbound-reclamation + scan halves depend on the persisted inventory Xcind deliberately omits (SA-0009). Document `prune` as the gc-equivalent.

gaps.capability: Port CLI: `port assign <port> <workspace>/<app>/<service>` — manual host-port pin (advanced escape hatch)
gaps.scind_promise: `scind port assign <port> <workspace>/<app>/<service>` — Manually assign a port (advanced usage).
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-proxy has release (:455) and prune (:477) but no `assign`. Ports are auto-assigned by lib/xcind/xcind-assigned-lib.bash. This is the only Scind port command with NO corresponding Xcind scope decision or design note explaining the omission.
gaps.recommendation: Decide: keep auto-only (and mark Scind's `port assign` as advanced/optional in the reconciliation), or add a pin/assign escape hatch to xcind-proxy. Genuinely absent with no rationale on record. Low urgency.

gaps.capability: Workspace-wide lifecycle orchestration (`workspace up/down/restart` across all apps; `scind up/down/ps` aliases)
gaps.scind_promise: `scind workspace up`: For each application (or specified apps): resolve active flavor; execute docker compose up -d. Plus workspace down/restart and `scind up` alias. up: 'bring the environment into existence'; down: 'tear down'.
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-workspace has init/status/list/register/forget only — NO up/down/restart. Lifecycle mutation is per-app via `xcind-compose up -d`/`down`, forwarded transparently to docker compose against the single cwd-detected app. No command brings a whole workspace up/down in one call; the user cd's into each app. ADR-0010 up/down VERB semantics themselves MATCH (Xcind inherits them natively from docker compose).
gaps.recommendation: HIGHEST-VALUE reconsider candidate. Consistent with Xcind's 'slim per-app compose wrapper' + stateless positioning, so DELIBERATELY-DEFERRED → P7. BUT `xcind-workspace status` already enumerates all apps, so a thin `xcind-workspace up/down` loop over app dirs would be a natural, high-utility addition — flag for a product decision, not pure divergence.

gaps.capability: `workspace clone` — clone application repos from repository URLs declared in workspace config
gaps.scind_promise: `scind workspace clone`: Reads repository URLs from workspace.yaml; clones each application to its configured path; skips apps that already exist; skips path: .; aborts on clone failure.
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-workspace subcommands are init/status/list/register/forget; no `clone` and no `repository` field in Xcind's .xcind.sh config schema. Xcind workspaces are pre-existing directories the user brings — there is no repo-manifest to clone from.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Out of scope for the slim-wrapper model (no repository-URL concept). No action unless a repo-manifest feature is later desired.

gaps.capability: Port Type Plugins — tcp/postgresql SNI-based TCP routing + a `protocol` port attribute for pluggable proxies
gaps.scind_promise: Port Type Plugins (Future): plugin system where protocols register handlers; protocol: postgresql; plugins generate Traefik TCP routers + SNI rules. port-types: 'proxied | tcp, postgresql, etc. | SNI-based TCP proxy (future)'.
gaps.evidence: Explicitly '(future)' in Scind's OWN canon (roadmap.md:8, port-types.md:9,32) and DROPPED from Xcind's roadmap (engineering/product/roadmap.md keeps only Application Dependencies + Health Checks). Xcind parse-entry accepts only type={proxied,assigned} and tls — no 'protocol' key (xcind-proxy-lib.bash:612-637); proxy routing is HTTP/HTTPS-only via Traefik web/websecure. Note: Xcind's http/https distinction is derived from TLS mode (mechanism difference, not a gap); only the tcp/SNI extension is the deferred capability.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Permanent Xcind subset decision (dropped from roadmap, stronger than merely unbuilt). Both projects defer; track as a shared future feature. No Xcind action.

gaps.capability: Application Dependencies (depends_on startup ordering within a workspace)
gaps.scind_promise: Application Dependencies (Future): add dependency ordering: workspace.applications.backend.depends_on: [shared-db].
gaps.evidence: No 'depends_on' in bin/ or lib/. Explicitly '(Future)' in Scind roadmap AND retained as planned future work on Xcind's own roadmap (engineering/product/roadmap.md:5-11 'Dependency ordering or startup sequencing within a workspace'). Both defer; Xcind KEEPS it planned.
gaps.recommendation: Low urgency — both defer, Xcind still plans it. Keep on Xcind backlog; no divergence (not a P7 permanent-divergence item, just shared future work).

gaps.capability: Shared Volumes (workspace-level volume definitions mountable into multiple applications)
gaps.scind_promise: Shared Volumes (Future): workspace-level volume definitions that can be mounted into multiple applications.
gaps.evidence: No 'shared volume'/'external volume' orchestration in bin/ or lib/. '(Future)' in Scind roadmap and absent from Xcind's roadmap entirely (dropped, like Port Type Plugins).
gaps.recommendation: → P7 as a permanent subset decision (dropped from Xcind roadmap). No Xcind action.

gaps.capability: Health Checks (Docker healthcheck readiness gating before dependents start)
gaps.scind_promise: Health Checks (Future): integration with Docker health checks to wait for readiness.
gaps.evidence: No health-check readiness-gating logic in bin/ or lib/. '(Future)' in Scind roadmap and retained as planned future work on Xcind's roadmap (engineering/product/roadmap.md:13-17).
gaps.recommendation: Low urgency — both defer, Xcind still plans it. Keep on Xcind backlog; no divergence.

gaps.capability: Port/service Visibility (public/protected) surfaced via Docker labels for external tool integration
gaps.scind_promise: Each port can have a visibility of public or protected ... included in the generated Docker labels (scind.export.<name>.proxy.<protocol>.visibility=...), enabling external tools (such as Servlo) to distinguish public and protected services.
gaps.evidence: parse-entry accepts only 'type' and 'tls'; a 'visibility' key fails fast as an unknown attribute (xcind-proxy-lib.bash:632-635), and test/test-xcind-proxy.sh:721-723 asserts exactly "unknown XCIND_PROXY_EXPORTS attribute 'visibility'". Xcind's docker-labels spec contains zero 'visibility' occurrences. This is a tested, ENFORCED omission — the strongest form of deliberate scope-out.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Note it is test-enforced (visibility is an error, not silently ignored). Reopens only if external-tool (Servlo-style) integration is later desired. Cross-references the docker-labels DIVERGED row (correspondence-map: P3 for the label-schema conflict).

gaps.capability: Customizable %VAR% template surface (workspace-level hostname/alias/project-name/apex templates + extra vars %APPLICATION_FLAVOR%, %SERVICE_PORT%, %WORKSPACE_NETWORK%, etc.)
gaps.scind_promise: workspace.templates: {hostname, alias, project-name, hostname-apex, alias-apex} customizable at workspace level with variables %PROXY_DOMAIN% %WORKSPACE_NAME% %WORKSPACE_NETWORK% %APPLICATION_NAME% %APPLICATION_FLAVOR% %EXPORTED_SERVICE% %SERVICE_NAME% %SERVICE_PORT% %SERVICE_PROTOCOL%.
gaps.evidence: Xcind covers hostname/router templating via XCIND_APP_URL_TEMPLATE + XCIND_ROUTER_TEMPLATE with {key} placeholders rendered by __xcind-render-template (xcind-proxy-lib.bash, xcind-workspace-lib.bash:11-99), but exposes a NARROWER, non-user-customizable variable set and no user-editable alias/project-name templates; %APPLICATION_FLAVOR% is moot (no flavors, SA-0008).
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Xcind's template surface is intentionally narrower; on-demand JSON introspection substitutes for the manifest-driven template exposure. Reconsider only if user-customizable alias/project-name templates are wanted.

gaps.capability: Context-detection UX: 'Using workspace: X (from ...)' feedback, dedicated exit code 5, and 'Available workspaces/apps' discovery hints on failure
gaps.scind_promise: When context is detected, commands indicate what was found: 'Using workspace: dev (from ../workspace.yaml)'. On failure: 'Error: No application context detected. ... Available apps in dev: frontend, backend, shared-db'. Exit code 5: Context detection failed.
gaps.evidence: engineering/specs/context-detection.md:40-56 — Xcind emits a generic 'Error: No .xcind.sh found. Are you inside an xcind-managed application?' with exit code 1; no 'Using workspace:' feedback, no 'Available workspaces/apps' suggestions, no dedicated exit code 5. (This also absorbs the Scind reference/appendices/cli/error-messages.md catalog: Xcind has no error/exit-code catalog.)
gaps.recommendation: Low-value UX polish; not a correctness risk. Xcind's flat exit-1 + generic error is functional. Add discoverability hints / differentiated exit codes opportunistically, or record as an accepted CANON difference. Rolls up the Scind error-messages appendix (see SA-0025).

gaps.capability: Universal output-format / verbosity flags: --yaml, --quiet/-q (names-only), --verbose/-v, --color across all commands
gaps.scind_promise: Global Flags: --workspace, --app, --quiet/-q, --verbose/-v, --json, --yaml, --color. --quiet: Status commands output just the value; List commands output names only, one per line.
gaps.evidence: Xcind provides `--json` broadly (xcind-config/proxy/workspace/application) but no `--yaml`, no `--quiet`/`-q` names-only mode, no `--verbose`/`-v`, no `--color` flag (engineering/reference/cli.md). Partly a framework difference (Cobra global-flag inheritance vs per-binary Bash parsers, EXPECTED) — but --yaml output and --quiet names-only are language-independent CLI capabilities Scind promises and Xcind lacks.
gaps.recommendation: `--json` already covers most machine-readable needs. Add `--quiet` names-only + optionally `--yaml` if scripting ergonomics matter; otherwise mark as an accepted CANON difference. Low urgency.

gaps.capability: Explicit `workspace generate` command (pre-build/persist override files for inspection & control)
gaps.scind_promise: `scind workspace generate`: Generates .generated/*.override.yaml files; updates .generated/manifest.yaml. Generation happens automatically during workspace up; use this command for explicit control.
gaps.evidence: No standalone `generate` subcommand; xcind-compose runs registered GENERATE hooks on-the-fly each invocation (engineering/reference/cli.md:16-20), nothing persisted to pre-build. `xcind-config --generate-docker-compose-configuration` exists for explicit inspection (ADR-0012). Facet of SA-0010.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Xcind generates via the hook pipeline every run (nothing persisted), so a separate generate command is unnecessary; --generate-* flags cover explicit inspection.

gaps.capability: `workspace destroy` command (full teardown: down --volumes, rm .generated, rm app dirs, release ports, deregister)
gaps.scind_promise: `scind workspace destroy`: Run workspace down --volumes; remove .generated/; prompt before removing app directories; remove workspace.yaml; release assigned ports; remove workspace from registry.
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-workspace has no `destroy`; only `forget PATH` drops a registry entry (engineering/reference/cli.md:338). In Xcind's stateless model destroy == `rm -rf` the dir + `xcind-workspace forget`; assigned-port release is covered by `xcind-proxy prune`. Facet of SA-0010.
gaps.recommendation: → P7. Stateless model: no dedicated command needed; optionally document the manual recipe (rm dir + forget + prune).

gaps.capability: Scind reference appendices: detailed CLI walkthroughs, error-message/exit-code catalog, complete config examples
gaps.scind_promise: Standalone appendices providing extended CLI walkthroughs, a catalog of CLI error messages + exit codes, and full worked configuration examples.
gaps.source: human product-call (#2: leave Scind as-is; known divergence, not a gap Xcind must close)
gaps.evidence: These are documentation-presentation artifacts, not runtime capabilities. CONFIRMED against Xcind's docs/ tree: real usage lives in Xcind's out-of-scope two-track USER docs (Diátaxis) — docs/reference/cli.md, docs/reference/configuration.md, docs/getting-started/{first-project,installation}.md, docs/guides/* (12 guides incl. proxy-setup, https-tls, host-gateway, env-files, override-files). Xcind folds worked examples inline rather than into separate appendices. Scind's appendices currently describe no code that Xcind must mirror.
gaps.recommendation: PER HUMAN PRODUCT-CALL: leave Scind's appendices as-is (no code exists behind them yet); Xcind demonstrates real usage under docs/ (Diátaxis) instead. This is a KNOWN DIVERGENCE, NOT a gap Xcind must close. The error-message/exit-code catalog is the only piece with no Xcind analog and is tracked as UX polish under SA-0021 — not required here. Record as a doc-presentation divergence → P7.

gaps.capability: scind-compose shell function + `scind compose-prefix` command (context-aware docker-compose passthrough with completion delegation)
gaps.scind_promise: `scind-compose` shell function: a context-aware passthrough to `docker compose`. Shell function + completion delegation achieves full docker compose completion 'for free'. `scind compose-prefix` outputs a docker compose command prefix that includes all necessary flags.
gaps.source: human product-call (#4: open question, unclear if Scind needs it)
gaps.evidence: bin/xcind-compose:1-76 is a REAL executable binary (not a sourced shell function); it resolves compose options internally via __xcind-prepare-app and `exec docker compose ...`. There is NO `compose-prefix` subcommand and no eval-of-prefix indirection. Xcind proved a standalone binary subsumes Scind's shell-function-over-compose-prefix mechanism (and gains docker-compose passthrough without a sourced function), but at the cost of the 'completion for free' delegation Scind's design buys.
gaps.recommendation: ESCALATE (human product-call): open question whether Scind still needs the scind-compose shell function + compose-prefix design at all, given Xcind's binary proved a viable alternative. This is NOT a settled divergence NOR a plain Xcind gap — it is a canon design question for the Scind side to resolve: keep the shell-function/compose-prefix architecture, adopt Xcind's binary approach, or support both. Route to human/P6 for a design decision; do not auto-classify.
